---
title: "3.2.3 Sensors"
subtitle: "Monitoring systems · Hardware P113-P114"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)

## Monitoring Systems

### Watching the process

Sensors are used in both <span class="term">monitoring</span> and <span class="term">control</span> applications.

There is a <span class="mark">subtle difference</span> between how these two methods work.

In a monitoring system, the microprocessor/computer has <span class="mark">no effect on what is being monitored</span>; it is simply <span class="term">watching</span> the process.

## Figure 3.50

### Monitoring path

![Figure 3.50 Monitoring and control systems using sensors](../../assets/3-hardware/3-2-3-sensors-monitoring-systems-p113-p114/Figure 3.50 Monitoring and control systems using sensors.png){fig-align="center" width="94%"}

::: {.figure-note}
Use the left-hand side of Figure 3.50 for monitoring: sensor input, ADC if necessary, comparison with stored values, then a warning message or alarm output.
:::

## Monitoring Path

### Stored values and warnings

The <span class="term">sensors</span> send signals to the microprocessor/computer.

The signals are converted to digital if necessary using an <span class="term">analogue to digital converter (ADC)</span>.

The computer/microprocessor analyses the data received by checking it against <span class="term">stored values</span>.

## Monitoring Path

### Outside the acceptable range

If new data is <span class="mark">outside the acceptable range</span>, a <span class="mark">warning message</span> is sent to a screen or an alarm is activated.

The important distinction is that the system warns or displays; it does not change the process being monitored.

## Examples of Monitoring

### Textbook examples

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">patient monitoring</span> in a hospital for vital signs such as heart rate and temperature
- monitoring of intruders in a <span class="term">burglar alarm</span> system
- checking the temperature levels in a car engine
- monitoring <span class="term">pollution levels</span> in a river
:::

## Figure 3.51

### Security system

![Figure 3.51 Security system](../../assets/3-hardware/3-2-3-sensors-monitoring-systems-p113-p114/Figure 3.51 Security system.png){fig-align="center" width="92%"}

::: {.figure-note}
Figure 3.51 concentrates on the <span class="term">sensor input</span> side of the security system.
:::

Note: compare this to Figure 3.9, which shows the security system in more detail.

## Security System 1/3

### Activation and sensor inputs

The system is activated by keying in a <span class="term">password</span> on a keypad.

::: {.tight-list}
- the <span class="term">infrared sensor</span> picks up the movement of an intruder in the building
- the <span class="term">acoustic sensor</span> picks up sounds such as footsteps or breaking glass
- the <span class="term">pressure sensor</span> picks up the weight of an intruder coming through a door or through a window
:::

## Security System 2/3

### ADC, sampling and stored values

The sensor data is passed through an <span class="term">ADC</span> if it is in an analogue form to produce digital data.

The computer/microprocessor samples the digital data coming from these sensors at a given frequency, for example <span class="mark">every 5 seconds</span>.

The data is compared with the <span class="term">stored values</span> by the computer/microprocessor.

## Security System 3/3

### Siren, lights and reset

If any incoming data values are <span class="mark">outside the acceptable range</span>, the computer sends a signal:

::: {.tight-list}
- to a <span class="term">siren</span> to sound the alarm
- to a light to start <span class="mark">flashing lights</span>
- through a <span class="term">DAC</span> if the devices need analogue values to operate them
:::

The alarm continues to sound, or the lights continue to flash, until the system is reset with a <span class="term">password</span>.

## Patient Monitoring 1/2

### Vital signs

A number of sensors are attached to the patient.

These measure <span class="term">vital signs</span> such as <span class="mark">temperature</span>, <span class="mark">heart rate</span> and <span class="mark">breathing rate</span>.

The sensors are all attached to a computer system and constantly send data back to the computer.

## Patient Monitoring 2/2

### Alarm or digital readout

The computer samples the data at frequent intervals.

The range of acceptable values for each parameter is keyed into the computer, and the values from the sensors are compared with those keyed in.

If anything is out of the acceptable range, a signal is sent to sound an alarm.

If data from the sensors is within range, the values are shown in graphical form on a screen and/or a <span class="term">digital readout</span> (digital read out).

Monitoring continues until the <span class="term">sensors are disconnected</span> from the patient.

## Monitoring Answer Pattern

### What to include

::: {.tight-list}
- sensor readings are sent to the computer/microprocessor
- analogue readings pass through an <span class="term">ADC</span> if necessary
- data is sampled at a stated frequency
- data is compared with stored or acceptable values
- if outside the acceptable range, a warning message, screen display, siren or alarm is triggered
- the system has <span class="mark">no effect on what is being monitored</span>
- in patient monitoring, monitoring continues until the sensors are disconnected
:::

## Classroom Check

### Keep monitoring separate from control

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- the sensors used and the physical property each one measures
- the <span class="term">ADC</span> step if the sensor data is analogue
- comparison with <span class="term">stored values</span> or an acceptable range
- the warning, alarm, screen output, siren or flashing lights
- the fact that monitoring only watches the process; it does not alter it
- the stopping condition where relevant, such as sensors being disconnected or the system being reset
:::

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)
